Commercial Apr 3, 2026

I’m A Flight Attendant: This Is What Happens When A Flight Is Overbooked

The good and the bad news.

Flight attendants manage a complex triage of passenger compensation, legal compliance, and crew logistics when overbooking occurs, often bearing the operational and emotional burden of resolving the situation.

See Simple Flying →

International Apr 3, 2026

Barents Sea Tied to Low Arctic Sea Ice

Patches of open water in the region contributed to low sea ice extent across the Arctic in March 2026, which tied with the lowest maximum observed in the satellite record.

Arctic sea ice decline in the Barents Sea region may create new polar aviation routes while increasing operational hazards from unpredictable weather patterns and emergency landing constraints.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

NASA’s Artemis II Mission Leaves Earth Orbit for Flight around Moon

For the first time in more than 50 years, astronauts on a NASA mission are bound to fly around the Moon after successfully completing a key burn of Orion’s main engine. With the approximately six-minute firing of the spacecraft’s service module engine on Thursday, known as the translunar injection...

The translunar injection burn confirms Orion's propulsion system reliability for deep-space missions, enabling NASA's pathway to lunar landings and future Mars exploration.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Artemis 2 heads to the moon

NASA’s Artemis 2 mission is on its way to the moon after a successful maneuver April 2.

Artemis 2's lunar trajectory demonstrates NASA's sustained commitment to deep-space exploration infrastructure that advances human spaceflight capabilities beyond Earth orbit.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Apr 3, 2026

Here’s How Much Space Premium Economy Adds Compared To Economy

Premium economy is booming on long-haul routes, but how much extra space do you really get, and does it actually change the flight experience?

Premium economy's modest 8-inch seat pitch increase and limited recline typically deliver 20-30% revenue uplift per seat despite marginal comfort gains, making it a high-margin cabin strategy rather than a transformative passenger experience.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

Operators must formally document their Safety Management System implementation to demonstrate FAA compliance, shifting accountability from reactive incident response to proactive risk management.

Aerospace Apr 3, 2026

Artemis II heads for the Moon after systems check and successful engine burn

NASA’s Artemis II crew is now officially on its way to the Moon after Orion’s service module engine…...

Successful Artemis II engine burn validates critical lunar transit propulsion capabilities essential for human deep-space exploration resumption.

See AeroTime Hub →

Military Apr 3, 2026

The Main Reason Why The F-35 So Expensive

The F-35 is expensive because it is a one-of-a-kind high end stealth fighter that also performs EV, command center, sensing, and other roles.

The F-35's multirole capability across combat, surveillance, and command functions drives costs beyond traditional single-mission fighter development models.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 2, 2026

Swift spacecraft reorientation buys time for reboost mission

NASA modified operations of an astrophysics spacecraft in a decaying orbit to buy more time for a mission later this year that will attempt to raise its orbit.

NASA's operational pivot demonstrates how proactive spacecraft attitude adjustments can extend mission viability while planning long-term orbital maintenance interventions.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Apr 2, 2026

Investigators: LaGuardia Controller May Have Stepped Away Before Deadly Crash

The incident quickly turned tragic.

Controller fatigue and understaffing patterns may have contributed to the incident, raising concerns about ATC workload management at major metropolitan airports.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Safety Apr 3, 2026

Safety Culture in Aviation SMS

How organizational culture shapes safety outcomes and what it takes to build a reporting culture that supports SMS.

Effective SMS depends on psychological safety that encourages reporting rather than punishment, directly correlating organizational trust levels to accident prevention rates.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Apr 2, 2026

The Real Reason Why Rolls-Royce Owns The Airbus A350 Engine Slot

A closer look at the host of advancements offered by the Trent XWB, and how it continues to develop.

Rolls-Royce's technological dominance in large turbofan efficiency, noise reduction, and material innovations secures its exclusive A350 engine partnership and creates a defensible competitive moat against future challengers.

See Simple Flying →

Air Cargo Apr 2, 2026

5 Reasons Why Airlines Still Fly The Boeing 747 Freighter

The Boeing 747 freighter remains valuable for its payload, cargo volume, nose-loading capability, and long-haul performance.

The 747F's nose-loading door and 140-ton payload capacity make it irreplaceable for oversized cargo routes where newer aircraft lack equivalent capability.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 2, 2026

Former Sierra Space CEO Tom Vice to lead Astrion

Huntsville-based defense contractor focuses on systems engineering and integration, and space mission assurance...

Sierra Space's leadership transition to a defense contractor signals potential strategic pivot toward government space contracts and mission-critical systems integration.

See SpaceNews →

Military Apr 2, 2026

Pentagon considers installing anti-drone lasers at sensitive US sites

The Pentagon is moving toward wider use of laser-based counter-drone systems at select US locations as concerns mount…...

Counter-drone laser deployments signal escalating threats to critical infrastructure that may reshape airspace security protocols around military installations.

See AeroTime Hub →

▶20:47
Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Policy & Safety Promotion

This video provides an overview of implementing a safety management system (SMS), specifically aligned with FAA Part 5 regulations. Join us as we explore Blue Skies Charter, a fictional company, and learn about general SMS requirements, safety policy development and safety promotion strategies. This video covers essential components like leadership commitment, gap analysis, safety objectives, emergency response planning and employee training. Whether you're a small charter operator or interested in aviation safety, this video offers valuable insights into building an effective SMS.

Small charter operators can leverage structured SMS implementation across safety policy, promotion, and employee training to meet FAA Part 5 compliance efficiently.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 2, 2026

17-Hour Nonstop Flights: 5 New Ultra-Long Routes By American, United & Delta

Which of the US Big Three carriers has the longest nonstop service? Find out here!

Ultra-long-haul expansion by US carriers reduces connection friction and captures premium international revenue, intensifying transatlantic competition amid aircraft efficiency gains.

See Simple Flying →

Business Aviation Apr 2, 2026

The Sky’s Greatest Dragon: Drake’s Luxurious 30-Year Old Boeing 767 Returns To The Skies

Despite its age, the Boeing 767 platform continues to offer strong structural reliability and adaptability for VIP transport roles.

Aging widebody aircraft demonstrate sustained value in high-end charter markets when properly maintained, creating secondary revenue opportunities for operators beyond primary fleet utilization.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 2, 2026

Optical terminals still a bottleneck in Pentagon’s proliferated constellation

GP Sandhoo: ‘From an optical communications terminal perspective, we’re not there yet on how many we need’...

The Pentagon's distributed satellite strategy is constrained by insufficient optical terminal manufacturing capacity, creating a critical supply-chain vulnerability for military space resilience.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Apr 2, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 2, 2026

A jet passes overhead as Artemis II begins its launch to the moon.

NASA's Artemis II lunar mission demonstrates continued integration of commercial aviation operations with active spaceport activities during high-stakes launches.

Regulatory Apr 3, 2026

FAA Part 5 SMS for Small Operators: Safety Risk Management

This video covers the safety management system (SMS) component safety risk management, which determines the need for, and adequacy of, new or revised risk controls based on the assessment of acceptable risk.

Small operators must establish documented risk assessment processes to justify control decisions, shifting from reactive compliance to proactive safety governance.

See NBAA (YouTube) →

General Aviation Apr 2, 2026

Avgas Prices Climbed in March

100LL, Jet A, UL94 and mogas all posted monthly increases nationwide.

Across-the-board fuel price increases signal tightening supply conditions that could pressure operating costs for all segments from general aviation through commercial operations.

See AVweb →

Military Apr 2, 2026

US sends EA-37B Compass Call into Iran fight in first known combat deployment

The US military has introduced a potent new weapon into the Iran fight with the deployment of the…...

The EA-37B Compass Call's combat debut signals escalating electronic warfare capabilities in Middle East operations, with implications for modern air defense suppression tactics.

See AeroTime Hub →

Regulatory Apr 2, 2026

FAA Releases Part 141 Modernization Proposal

Report outlines proposals to alter certificated flight school approval, oversight and training technology.

FAA's modernization framework could accelerate pilot training timelines by enabling schools to integrate advanced simulation and competency-based progression rather than relying solely on traditional hour requirements.

See AVweb →

Commercial Apr 2, 2026

This Is Canada's Busiest Airport

A closer look at the most popular hub in the Great White North.

Toronto Pearson's dominance as Canada's primary international gateway reflects consolidated hub-and-spoke routing that shapes competitive dynamics for regional carriers and cross-border traffic patterns.

See Simple Flying →

Industry Apr 3, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across all aviation layers—not just aircraft technology—offers near-term capacity and emissions gains while research gaps remain in implementation scalability.

See MDPI →

Military Apr 2, 2026

Pentagon awards Raytheon $45 million for GPS ground system as program future is reassessed

The ‘unpriced change order’ supports satellite launches while officials reassess long-delayed ground system...

Raytheon's continued funding despite program delays signals Pentagon commitment to modernizing GPS infrastructure, though the 'unpriced' contract structure suggests uncertainty about total project scope and cost.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Apr 2, 2026

Here’s How Much Wider The Airbus A350 Cabin Is Compared To The Boeing 787 Dreamliner

The Airbus A350 cabin is 5 inches wider than the Boeing 787—here’s how that small difference transforms seat comfort and long-haul travel.

The A350's 5-inch cabin width advantage enables airlines to fit 10-abreast seating versus the 787's 9-abreast, directly impacting unit economics on long-haul routes.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 2, 2026

Artemis II Astronauts Launch to Moon

NASA’s Space Launch System rocket and Orion spacecraft lift off in this April 1, 2026, image. NASA’s Artemis II mission will take NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy on an approximately 10-day journey around the Moon and...

Artemis II's crewed lunar flyby in 2026 will validate human spaceflight systems critical for sustained deep-space operations and future planetary exploration infrastructure.

See NASA →

Regulatory Apr 2, 2026

Washington Repeals Aircraft Tax Before It Takes Effect

Measure enacted in 2025 is replaced with fuel tax and fee increases.

Washington's pivot from aircraft excise tax to fuel taxes and fees shifts the tax burden toward operational costs rather than aircraft purchases, potentially reducing purchase barriers for new operators.

See AVweb →

Military Apr 2, 2026

Global Combat Air Programme Awards First Contract with Edgewing

The first joint international contract has been awarded for the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP), focusing on key design and engineering activities. The GCAP International Government Organisation (GIGO), which represents the governments of the UK, Italy and Japan, awarded a £686 million (about...

The first GCAP contract signals tripartite commitment to collaborative fighter development, with design phase investment indicating multi-year procurement pipeline and industrial deepening among UK, Italian, and Japanese aerospace sectors.

See The Aviationist →

Safety Apr 2, 2026

How the Iran War Is Rewiring Avionics

Global Avionics Round-Up from Aircraft Value News (AVN) The latest Middle East conflict has done what decades of airline risk modeling feared but never fully priced: it has snapped the […]...

Regional conflicts are forcing airlines to rapidly reassess and reprice geopolitical risks that insurance models and route planning previously underestimated.

See Aviation Today →

Industry Apr 2, 2026

Silicon Over Steel: The Avionics Reset That Stole the Show at ISTAT Americas 2026

Global Avionics Round-Up from Aircraft Value News (AVN) From my perspective in covering the show as a reporter, the biggest avionics story to emerge from ISTAT Americas 2026 wasn’t a […]...

Modern avionics manufacturers are shifting from traditional hardware-centric designs to software-defined architectures, fundamentally reducing lifecycle costs and enabling faster capability updates across airline fleets.

See Aviation Today →

Safety Apr 2, 2026

Climate Change Is Rewriting the Rules of Turbulence—and Cockpits Are Playing Catch-Up

Global Avionics Round-Up from Aircraft Value News (AVN) For decades, turbulence was treated as a known nuisance—unpredictable, occasionally dangerous, but broadly manageable within the design assumptions of modern aircraft. Engineers […]...

Clear-air turbulence intensity is increasing faster than aircraft design standards anticipated, requiring updated detection systems and structural reassessment.

See Aviation Today →

Military Apr 2, 2026

Army’s SkyFoundry Plan To Build Own Cheap Drones Has ‘Evolved,’ Official Notes Commercial Involvement

The Army’s SkyFoundry initiative to manufacture thousands of its own cheap drones on a monthly basis has recently “evolved,” with an official telling sister publication Defense Daily the program is “100 […]...

The Army's shift toward commercial partnership for SkyFoundry suggests a strategic pivot from in-house manufacturing to leveraging existing industry capacity, potentially accelerating drone production timelines while reducing development costs.

See Aviation Today →

Military Apr 2, 2026

With an Eye To CCA Increment 2, Talon IQ Conducts Test Flight

A “Talon IQ” Model 437 aircraft by Northrop Grumman‘s Scaled Composites has flown a mission autonomy test flight using the company’s Prism software and Hivemind software by Shield AI, the companies […]...

Northrop Grumman's autonomous flight integration with Shield AI's Hivemind demonstrates practical progress toward Defense Department's CCA increment capabilities for unmanned collaborative operations.
